coredata.dc:title: Considerations for the sizing and structure of the battery pack for a direct-hybrid-electric aircraft
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.dc:creator: Hoenicke, Pia
Willich, Caroline
coredata.dc:description: The development of hydrogen fuel-cell-based aircraft has received a lot of attention recently. Components like the battery need to be carefully designed to meet power and safety requirements while minimizing weight to enable longer flight ranges. Experimental data from three different commercially available lithium-ion batteries with different cathode materials and housing was used to parameterize an equivalent circuit battery model. The model with the three datasets was integrated into a system model for the powertrain of a direct hybrid and used to find the required battery configuration for an exemplary flight profile with cells connected in series and parallel for all three battery types. The resulting battery configurations were compared with respect to flight reserve and weight. It was found that due to current and voltage restraints, the most suitable and lightest configuration was the one based on lithium nickel cobalt aluminum oxide (NCA) cylindrical cells despite the fact that they were not the type of battery with the highest specific energy on cell level. The NCA had a specific energy on cell level of 126 Wh/kg at 23 °C with a 1 C-discharge, compared to the lithium nickel manganese cobalt oxide (NMC) battery with 232 Wh/kg and lithium iron phosphate (LFP) with 79 Wh/kg. The battery systems that met all voltage and flight reserve requirements however resulted in a system weight of 200 kg for the NMC cell, while the system using NCA cells was only 183 kg.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Hybrid-electric aircraft
Battery configuration
Batteries for aviation

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Required system power during different flight phases for an exemplary flight profile of a 4-seater aircraft (Hy4).
Schematic of model structure.
Equivalent circuit model for the battery simulations.
Comparison of measured 2 C characteristic discharge curve at 23 °C with simulations parametrized with EIS and different current pulse measurements.
Schematic of the battery system model with controlled sources and feed backs.
Measured and simulated polarization curve for a HD12 Stack (Cummins Inc., USA; formerly Hydrogenics).
Schematic of the fuel cell model in matlab.
Efficiency map of the motor-inverter system.
Schematic of the matlab model for the electric motor.
Schematic of the direct hybrid architecture with fuel cell, battery, power management system and two inverter-motor systems.
Current, voltage and power of the fuel cell, battery, the two inverters and mechanical shaft power for a hybrid with a battery pack with 80 serial and 3 parallel NCA battery cells.
Current, voltage and power of the fuel cell, battery, the two inverters and mechanical shaft power for a hybrid with a battery pack with 80 serial and 5 parallel NCA battery cells.

Current, voltage and power of the fuel cell, battery, the two inverters and mechanical shaft power for a hybrid with a battery pack with 80 serial and 5 parallel NCA battery cells during a flight including a go around after a failed landing attempt.
Battery losses during flight for the different battery configurations in the hybrid.
Comparison of battery types used for parametrization.

row.entry: NMC lithium nickel manganese cobalt oxide)

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Flight reserve as SOC value after climb and after recharging for the different battery configurations in the hybrid.
Flight reserve as SOC value after go-around for the different battery configurations in the hybrid.

head.ce:title: Considerations for the sizing and structure of the battery pack for a direct-hybrid-electric aircraft

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute for Energy Conversion and -Storage, Ulm University, 89081 Ulm, Germany
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Institute for Energy Conversion and -Storage, Ulm University, 89081 Ulm, Germany

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The development of hydrogen fuel-cell-based aircraft has received a lot of attention recently. Components like the battery need to be carefully designed to meet power and safety requirements while minimizing weight to enable longer flight ranges. Experimental data from three different commercially available lithium-ion batteries with different cathode materials and housing was used to parameterize an equivalent circuit battery model. The model with the three datasets was integrated into a system model for the powertrain of a direct hybrid and used to find the required battery configuration for an exemplary flight profile with cells connected in series and parallel for all three battery types. The resulting battery configurations were compared with respect to flight reserve and weight. It was found that due to current and voltage restraints, the most suitable and lightest configuration was the one based on lithium nickel cobalt aluminum oxide (NCA) cylindrical cells despite the fact that they were not the type of battery with the highest specific energy on cell level. The NCA had a specific energy on cell level of 126 Wh/kg at 23 °C with a 1 C-discharge, compared to the lithium nickel manganese cobalt oxide (NMC) battery with 232 Wh/kg and lithium iron phosphate (LFP) with 79 Wh/kg. The battery systems that met all voltage and flight reserve requirements however resulted in a system weight of 200 kg for the NMC cell, while the system using NCA cells was only 183 kg.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Overcoming climate change by reducing CO2 emissions is one of the main tasks these days. The aviation industry adds up to about 3 % of the CO2 emissions in the European Union [1] and with non-CO2 effects considered the climate impacts adds up to up to 8 % [1]. These emissions are expected to grow strongly over the next years due to an international increase in air travel.

In order to meet climate goals as for example defined by the European commission in the Paris Agreement, new technologies are required, as a mere improvement in conventional technologies cannot solve the problem [2]. Sustainable aviation fuels are an environmentally friendly option, but production rates will not be able to meet the entire demand of the aviation industry [3].

Electric aircraft can enable emission less flight. However, using batteries as energy carriers is limited in aviation due to the low specific energy of batteries, which is currently between 200 and 300 Wh/kg [4]. The needed specific energy for an aircraft varies according to literature between 300 and 1280 Wh/kg dependent on the aircraft size and flight distance [5–10]. The range of battery driven planes is therefore limited to short distances [7] since the range is proportional to the specific energy [11]. Further development with respect to safety and reliability of batteries is also required [3]. However, battery powered aircraft have already demonstrated flights e.g. the company Eviation built a 9-seater aircraft called Alice with lithium-ion NMC battery cells from the company Kokam forming a battery pack with 820 kWh and 3720 kg weight [12,13]. The company Pipistrel even built the first European Union Aviation Safety Agency (EASA) certified all-electric two-seater aircraft called Velis electro using cylindrical lithium ion batteries with the cathode material NMC [14,15]. The battery system has an energy of 12.4 kWh and can fly around 50 min with a flight reserve of 20 min [16]. There are several more, like the Accel aircraft developed by Rolls Royce, the Eflyer developed by the company ByeAerospace or the X-57 Maxwell developed by the National Aeronautics and Space Administration (NASA) [14,15,17–24]. However, there is little information on the exact battery types, configurations and sizing of each of these aircraft and their flight ranges are typically short.

The hybridization with another energy carrier can greatly enhance the range of a prospective electric aircraft. The hybridisation of batteries with gas turbines are seen as having the potential to reduce the climate impact of aviation in the short term already by 2030 while aircraft using hydrogen fuel cells are a promising option on a slightly longer timeframe (around 2040) [25]. Hydrogen is seen as having a great potential to reduce the climate impact of aviation of up to 90 % [26] if fuel cells are used to convert the hydrogen into electricity. A hybrid electric aircraft with hydrogen fuel cells and a battery is therefore a promising possibility to reduce not only CO2 but also NOx, particles, sulfur components and hydrocarbon emissions in aviation. Hydrogen is an option for aircraft, especially for longer distances where batteries reach their limits, since H2 has a specific energy of 33.314 Wh/kg, which is around 100 times higher than the specific energy of batteries [27]. There are some research projects with aircraft powered only by fuel cells, like Hydrogenius developed by the university of Stuttgart, the Antares 20E developed by DLR or HyFlyer developed by ZeroAvia [28–34]. Other approaches, like the Hy4 use fuel cell-battery-hybrids due to the high power demands at the take-off and climb phase [35,36]. Moreover, the integration of a battery allows for the possibility of recuperation.

Whether as part of a hybrid or as a battery-only powered aircraft, the battery must be designed to match the requirements of the application. Battery properties vary depending on battery chemistry as well as housing. Lithium ion batteries are the most promising battery type for aircraft due to their high specific energy [37] and high technology readiness level (TRL) [38,39]. The behavior of lithium ion cells is mainly influenced by the used cathode materials and the type of housing. Pouch cells are only covered by a film and therefore tend to have the lowest weight, while both cylindrical cells and prismatic cells have a solid housing and tend to be heavier [40]. Cylindrical housing cells however are promising due to their commercial standard and the highest energy density on cell level [40]. When assessing the suitability of different types of lithium ion batteries for aviation it can be found that with respect to specific energy, the cathode materials NCA and NMC are the most promising [41], while LFP cathode materials offer the advantage of being very safe [42].

Vratny et al. examine the possibility to integrate the battery discharge characteristics into the aircraft sizing loop for a battery only plane to estimate the battery capacity required for a mission as well as its mass [43]. Avanzini et al. developed a sizing procedure and demonstrated it for small-size unmanned battery powered aerial vehicle for maximum endurance and maximum range based on a lithium-polymer (Li-Po) battery model validated with experimental data at different power levels [44]. They do not consider different flight phases, only cruise performance with 3 battery packs with different number of cells in series. Sripad et al. compare battery packs of 5 electric vertical take-off and landing aircraft (EVTOL) with respect to specific power and energy, however no information on the specific battery configuration is given [45]. Tariq et al. compare two battery systems for more electric aircraft (MEA) with respect to weight and cost [46]. They consider a LFP system with serially connected cells and a nickel cadmium (NiCd) system with cells connected in series and parallel for a 270 V bus system for power demand during start and emergency in MEA.

The aircraft considered in this paper is the regional four-seater Hy4 with a take-off power of approximately 90 kW which flew as a flying test bench in 2020 [35,47,48]. The hybrid power train consists of two motor-inverter systems for redundancy reasons, a power management control unit to control the power flow and a battery or fuel-cell-battery energy storage to power the aircraft. In order to make the best use of the high specific energy of hydrogen and enable a long range the fuel cell has to have enough power to run the aircraft during the low power phases like cruise.

During phases like take-off and climb, during which the power demand is higher, the battery can boost the fuel cell and both the fuel cell and the battery are providing the power required for take-off. The power demand during different flight phases of an exemplary flight can be seen in Fig. 1 . During descent and landing the power demand for propulsion is very low and the battery can even be charged.

The paper examines the necessary battery configuration for meeting the power demand and safety requirements for three different battery types. Based on the voltage-current characteristics the necessary number of battery cells in series and parallel is determined and the resulting battery system weight is assessed with the aim to find the most suitable battery configuration for the examined case.

In order to determine what battery configuration and battery type is the most suited for an application it is advantageous to compare and analyze the system with the help of a simulation of the power train rather than by building up different battery systems, integrating them into the aircraft system and testing them.

To be able to represent the behavior of the power train each subsystem is simulated in Matlab Simulink Version R2021a. The fuel cells, battery cells and inverter-motor-systems were implemented by mathematical equations. The electrical physical behavior of the battery system, fuel cell system and the power management system called PMCD (power management control and delivery) were implemented using the Simscape library. Voltage and current sensors as well as sinks and mathematical physical converters were used for feedback loops to connect the two different kinds of models. The subsystem simulations are parameterized and validated with experimental data. The subsystem models are then combined into a system model which is then used to evaluate different battery system configurations.

For the simulation of the battery system, experimental data was used to parameterize and validate a cell level model implemented in Matlab Simulink Simscape library, which was then in turn used to represent the behavior of a battery system by connecting various cells in series and parallel. The battery system model could then be integrated into a model of the drive train, which was also implemented in Matlab Simulink as shown schematically in Fig. 2 .

The battery is simulated on cell level by an equivalent circuit as shown in Fig. 3 . Equivalent circuit models still represent physical meaning but simplify the calculations. An equivalent circuit model is chosen since it shows the best compromise regarding accuracy, computational effort and necessary simulation parameters compared to empirical and physical models. The equivalent circuit model consists of a voltage source, a serial resistance and two resistance-capacitance (RC)-circuits. The voltage source represents the open circuit voltage (OCV) of the cell dependent on the state of charge (SOC). The serial resistance represents the ohmic resistance of the electrode and electrolyte. The two RC circuits represent the interfaces between each electrode and the electrolyte [49].

Each component of the equivalent circuit model was parameterized by measurements on real battery cells Three commercially available battery types that are specifically made for aerospace applications were chosen and tested experimentally as described previously [50]. They differed in their cathode materials as well as their housing. The properties of the three different battery types can be seen in Table 1 .

The OCV voltage source in the model was parameterized using OCV measurements obtained either by galvanostatic intermittent titration technique (GITT) or Quasi-OCV measurement. The resistances and capacitances of the equivalent circuit model were parameterized by fitting them to data obtained by electrical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) and current pulses at different SOC levels and temperatures. The two measuring techniques were compared to each other. It was found that the parameters obtained from the current pulse measurements with high current values showed the best agreement with the measured curves as can be seen in Fig. 4 . The agreement of the model that was parameterized using EIS showed a stronger deviation from the measurements than when 50 A current pulses were used. Due to the higher accuracy the model parameter obtained from the current pulses were integrated into the battery model.
The measurements and the determination of the parameters of the equivalent circuit model led to a parameter set for different state of charge for each battery type.

To scale up the battery cell behavior to a battery system behavior, several simulated cells were connected in series and in parallel. For obtaining the system voltage the cell voltage was multiplied by the number of serial cells while the total system current was divided by the number of parallel cells to get the single cell current for the simulation. The battery system is implemented as a controlled voltage source from Matlab's Simscape library as shown in Fig. 5 , which is connected to the model of the aircraft power train.

The fuel cell is simulated based on the measured polarization curve of one fuel cell stack of type HD12 (Cummins Inc., USA; formerly Hydrogenics) [51]. The constants a, b, c and d of an exponential curve as given in Eq. (1) are fitted to the measured points of the fuel cell. The measured data points and the fitted curve can be seen in Fig. 6 . The fuel cell system voltage is calculated by multiplying the number of stacks in series with the current dependent voltage as can be seen in Eq. (2) and shown in Fig. 7 . For representing the fuel cell behavior in the model, the fitted curve feeds a controlled voltage source from Matlab's Simscape library which is connected to the rest of the power train.
(1)
V FuelCell
= a ∗
e
b ∗
I FuelCell
+ c ∗
e
d ∗
I FuelCell
(2)
V FuelCellSystem
=
V Stack
∙
n Stack

The motor-inverter-system is simulated based on an efficiency map of a motor from the company Compact Dynamics (Germany). The efficiency of the motor-inverter system was measured on a testrig for different speed and torque values. The data can be seen in Fig. 8 . Since no experimental data was available for very low rpm, an efficiency of 50 % for 1 rpm was added to enable the model to ramp up the speed. 50 % efficiency at 1 rpm was used to be sure it is lower than all other efficiency values. However, since the motor speed ramps up very fast, the efficiency below 500 rpm does not have a big influence on the simulation result.

In the model the motor is represented by a current sink as a load as shown in Fig. 9 . The DC power of the inverter is calculated by dividing the required mechanical power by the motor-inverter-system efficiency as shown in Eq. (3). From this DC power the load current is calculated by dividing the power by the simulated DC bus voltage produced by fuel cell and/or battery as shown in Eq. (4).
(3)
P DC
=
2 πMn
η Motor
η Inverter
η Coupling
(4)
I Load
=
P DC
V
DC Bus

The described models for each component were combined into an aircraft power train model. For the analysis of a system that includes the hybridization of a fuel cell and a battery the approach of a direct hybrid is used. In the considered direct hybrid a model of an unregulated power management connection was used as described previously [52,53]. The architecture of the system can be seen in Fig. 10 . The main advantages of this power management system are the good trade-off between flexibility and control effort as well as the high safety due to the inherent redundancy.

In this direct hybrid the fuel cell and the battery are connected to the inverter motor system via the power management system. The power management system consists of several switches, diodes, fuses and connections as can be seen schematically in Fig. 10. The switches allow to connect or disconnect the fuel cell and battery from the rest of the power train at will and different operational modes can be chosen depending on the power demand during a certain flight phase. For example, low power demand phases like cruise can be flown drawing power only from the fuel cell and for high power phases like take-off or climb the battery can be connected as well. If very little power is needed for propulsion the battery can also be charged from the fuel cell [52].

For the model, all components are simulated by the Simscape library of Matlab Simulink. Losses within the power train, for example due to resistances in cables and diodes, were also included and the model was validated previously using real flight data.

To determine the required size of the fuel cell and the batteries for the hybrid, the aircraft power profile as shown in Table 2 was used as a starting point. The cruise power of the aircraft is 40 kW and the fuel cell was chosen to be able to provide the entire cruise power by itself. In order to meet the cruise power demand, 4 fuel cells stacks (12 kW each) were used in series to simulate the fuel cell.

Since the battery and fuel cells are connected in a direct hybrid the voltage level of the fuel cell has a direct implication for the battery system, since the voltage levels of battery and fuel cell have to match [54]. The nominal OCV of the four fuel cell stacks connected in series is 480 V. The lowest voltage the four fuel cell stacks can provide at maximum power is approx. 240 V. In a direct hybrid the battery voltage needs to be between the maximum and minimum voltage of that of the fuel cell. Therefore, the battery OCV was chosen to be 70 % of the nominal fuel cell OCV resulting in a maximum battery voltage of 336 V (70 % of 480 V). This voltage defines the number of battery cells that have to be connected in series. To now meet the required take-off power of 90 kW total (50 kW of which have to be provided by the battery), the batteries can be connected in parallel as well and the number of parallel battery branches required can be determined from Eq. (5). Here Vcell is the OCV voltage at 100 % SOC, Icell is the maximum allowed battery cell current, nserial is the number of serial cells and nparallel is the number of parallel branches.
(5)
P Battery
=
V Cell
∙
n serial
∙
I Cell
∙
n parallel

Since the three different batteries that were used differ in their voltage-current behavior, different cell configurations are required to meet the power and voltage demands. With this preliminary sizing approach, the battery systems with serial and parallel branches as shown in Table 2 are calculated. The table also includes the resulting battery system weight. In order to determine the battery system weight, a decrease in specific energy from cell to pack of 30 % was assumed based on literature values [55–57]. The weight was calculated using Eq. (6) from the number of cells in series and in parallel, their weight, which was determined by weighing them and the mentioned factor for considering a lower specific energy on pack level than on cell level.
(6)
m
Battery system
=
m Cell
·
n serial
·
n parallel
· 1 / 0.7

ce:section.ce:section-title: Flight simulations and final sizing of the battery
ce:section.ce:para: To see how the systems designed in the previous chapter perform for the flight profile defined in Fig. 11 and Table 3 , simulations were done for all three battery configurations. The results were then examined whether the required power could be provided during every flight phase and whether any limits, like the maximum allowed current of a battery, were exceeded.

Fig. 11 shows the power, voltage and current from the fuel cell and the battery as well as at the inverters and mechanical shaft power for a battery pack with 80 serial and 3 parallel NCA cells during the flight time. The initial SOC of the battery was set to 90 % and the operational battery temperature was set to 23 °C. It can be seen that the system is capable to deliver the required power at each phase during the entire flight.

For take-off and climb the system is in hybrid mode and the fuel cell and the battery provide the power required for propulsion as well as the losses in the system. During the first 32 s of take-off, the fuel cell voltage is still sufficiently high that the power is provided by the fuel cell. Once the system voltage reaches the OCV of the battery, the power is shared between fuel cell and battery and the system voltage is defined by that of the battery. Once current is drawn from the battery, its SOC changes which results in a voltage decrease during take-off and climb and therefore a power share shift towards the fuel cell.

At the beginning of cruise, the battery is disconnected in order to supply the complete propulsive power from the fuel cell. Once the power demand for propulsion decreases during descent, the fuel cell can deliver more power than required for propulsion and it is possible to switch into charging mode. As the power drawn from the fuel cell decreases, its voltage rises above the OCV of the battery and the battery is charged from the fuel cell. Once the plane lands, the system can be switched off.

It can be seen from Fig. 11 that the maximum current drawn from the battery during take-off is 213 A. This is however above the maximum allowed current of 150 A as defined in Table 2. During the preliminary sizing the dependency of the direct hybrid voltage and the resulting fuel cell and battery currents was not sufficiently considered.

In order to ensure that the current limit of the batteries is not exceeded the number of batteries has to be adapted. In order to provide more current from the battery the number of parallel branches needs to be increased. A higher number of batteries in parallel also leads to a higher voltage since less polarization losses occur at the single cells. Therefore, it might also be possible to reduce the number of cells in series when more batteries are connected in parallel.

For the NCA cell it was found that it is necessary to increase the number of batteries in parallel from 3 to 5 to not exceed the limit. This increases the allowed current to 250 A as can be seen from Table 4 . Fig. 12 shows the simulated power, voltage and current from the fuel cell and the battery as well as at the inverters and mechanical shaft power for this new configuration. It can be seen from the figure that the power contribution from the fuel cell during climb is now slightly smaller than before due to the changed voltage levels. The voltage level in the direct hybrid depends on the battery voltage. The battery current is now higher (max. 217.8 A) to provide the required power, but the maximum allowed battery current is no longer exceeded.

The same procedure was followed for the other battery types. The flight mission was simulated for the preliminary sizing and the results were checked to determine whether modifications were necessary. For the battery configurations based on LFP and NMC cells a similar adaptation as for the NCA cell was necessary in order to meet all requirements during the flight profile. The preliminary configuration using NMC cells also exceeded the battery current and the number of parallel connections had to be increased from 7 to 9. The resulting new configuration can be seen in Table 4.

The preliminary configuration for the LFP cells was not even able to provide the energy required for take-off and climb. The preliminary LFP configuration was already completely discharged during the climb phase at second 495. In order to increase the energy stored in the battery a second branch was connected in parallel. The required energy could not be obtained by increasing the number of serial cells, due the voltage limits of the fuel cell. Since the current per cell was a lot lower in the configuration with 2 branches, the losses per cell were also lower. The lower losses lead to a higher operational voltage at a certain power. Therefore, less cells needed to be connected in series to meet the demand. The number of serial cells could be reduced from 96 to 86. For both the NCA and NMC cell no reduction in serial cells was possible, since a reduction led to the fuel cell current or the battery charging current limits being exceeded.

Table 2 and Table 4 also list the resulting weight of the battery system configurations based on the different cell types for the preliminary and the final sizing respectively. Since battery systems weight more than just the sum of the weight of the individual cells a decrease in specific energy from cell to pack of 30 % was assumed for all configurations based on values available in literature [55,56]. The weight was determined according to Eq. (6). It can be seen from Table 2 that the preliminary NMC system is the heaviest despite the pouch cell being the lightest on cell level. This is because more cells are needed in parallel (7 parallel branches) to enable the current required by the system. The preliminary NCA based configuration was the lightest of the three preliminary configurations. When comparing the weight of the three final configurations in Table 4, it can be seen that the NCA configuration is still the lightest, followed by the NMC configuration, while the LFP based system was the heaviest. In order to provide the required energy during the flight a second parallel branch had to be added to the preliminary LFP system which doubled the weight making it the heaviest system of all. The higher currents that can be drawn from the NCA cells in comparison to the NMC batteries made it possible to have fewer parallel branches for the NCA than for the NMC configurations which lead to the NCA system being the lightest despite the fact that the NMC cells had the lowest weight on cell level.
The results show that a detailed and careful sizing that considers the different battery behaviors is necessary for optimizing the performance and weight of the system.

Another point to consider when designing the battery pack is the required flight reserve at the end of the flight. According to Viswanathan [9], a flight reserve (which corresponds to the remaining SOC within the battery at the end of the flight) of 19 % is needed. The flight reserve is required in case a go around after a failed landing attempt is required.

Table 5 shows the remaining SOC for the battery configurations defined in Table 4 at the end of the climb phase when the battery is disconnected and also after recharging the cells during descent. It can be seen that all battery configurations had a sufficiently high flight reserve even without recharging. The NMC battery pack had the highest reserve while the LFP had the lowest. After recharging the batteries during descent, the SOC of the LFP based system has risen to 62 %, while the NCA and NMC systems reach an SOC of 50 % and 64 % respectively. The increase in SOC due to recharging during descent was therefore highest for the LFP based system.

To assess the system performance during a go-around an adapted flight profile as shown in Fig. 13 was used and all three battery configurations were simulated. The go-around profile has another take-off and short climb phase added after the first descent before the second descent phase starts. A second cruise phase was omitted in this example, since the fuel cell would provide the power for that.

Fig. 13 shows the currents, voltages and powers for the NCA battery pack with 80 cells in series and 5 cells in parallel as defined in Table 4. It can be seen that the battery voltage just before the go around is with 291 V lower than at the beginning of the first take-off where the voltage was 326.6 V and the second take-off takes place at a lower system voltage, too. As a result, the fuel cell contributed more current during that second take-off. Neither the maximum fuel cell current, nor the maximum battery current was exceeded as the maximum battery current was 218 A and the maximum fuel cell current was 191 A and the power demand could be met. The charging current during decent reached a maximum value of 85.2 A which is below the maximum allowed value, too. After the second take-off and climb phase the remaining SOC of the batteries is 7 % as can be seen in Table 6 .

The same flight profile was simulated for the battery configurations with NMC cells and LFP cells. For the NMC battery configuration the overall behavior was similar to the NCA configuration and the power demand as well as all current limits were met and the remaining SOC at the end of the second climb phase was 44 %.

The system configuration determined for the LFP cells however could not meet all requirements. The system still managed to provide the required power, but the fuel cell current during the second take-off phase reached 204.9 A which is above the limit of 200 A. The SOC at the end of the second climb phase was 4 % in the case of the LFP battery configuration.

In order to prevent the system from exceeding the maximum fuel cell current during the go-around flight profile, it would be necessary to increase the battery voltage by increasing the number of cells in series. The resulting higher battery voltage would ensure a higher system voltage and a larger contribution to the overall power demand of the battery. However, the higher current drawn from the battery then leads to an even deeper discharge and therefore overdischarging during the second climb which leads the system to fail the mission. It would therefore be required to increase the number of cells in parallel by adding another parallel cell branch (so at least 86 more cells) to the system. Since the battery configuration with LFP cells as defined in 4 is already the heaviest in comparison to the configurations with the other cell types, it makes this cell type even more unsuited for the application.

Since efficiency is also an important aspect to consider, the losses that occurred in each battery configuration from Table 4 were determined as well for the flight profile shown in Fig. 1. The internal losses of the batteries were calculated according to Eq. (7), were VOCV is the open circuit voltage of the battery, Vcell the actual operating voltage and I the current that is drawn from the battery.
(7)
P Loss
=
V OCV
−
V cell
· I
The losses for the three different battery setups are plotted in Fig. 14 against the flight time of the simulated flight profile. It can be seen that the values for the losses differ for the different battery configurations considered. This is due to the different characteristic curves, but also the different current rates for the single cells in the different systems. The losses for all battery configurations are highest during take-off and climb, even though the exact time at which the losses peak differs. During cruise the losses for all batteries are zero, since the battery is not providing power in that phase. The highest loss value for the NCA battery configuration during take-off was 10.1 W, for the LFP configuration it was 18.2 W and for the NMC setup it was 6.4 W. For all battery configurations the losses during charging were lower than during discharge, due to the lower currents.

Integrated over the entire simulated flight time, the total losses were 5059 J for the NCA battery. The highest losses of 12,105 J occur for the LFP battery, while the NMC battery showed the lowest loss with only 3060 J. The NMC battery had the lowest losses since each single cell in the system delivers a lower current in the NMC configuration compared to both the NCA and LFP configurations. In the NMC configurations 9 branches deliver the power in parallel, while in the NCA system 5 parallel branches provide the power and in the LFP system it is only 2 parallel branches.

The battery system required for a direct hybrid consisting of a fuel cell, a battery, power management module and two inverter-motor systems in a small 4-seater aircraft was sized based on experimental data of three different battery types. Experimental data obtained by measuring commercially available batteries that differed in their cathode materials (NCA, NMC and LFP) as well as in their housing types was used to validate an equivalent circuit battery model. The battery model was used to examine the necessary configurations of single cells in series and parallel to define the necessary battery configurations in order for the aircraft to successfully fly a defined flight profile. The fuel cell was sized to provide the propulsive power during cruise, while the battery system and the fuel cell provided the power required for take-off and climb together.

The battery configurations required to meet all current, voltage and power requirements of the system differed for the different cell types. A preliminary sizing based on the maximum current and voltage of each battery type resulted in set of three battery configurations with different numbers of cells in series and parallel for the three different battery types. However, when simulating the entire flight profile for the hybrid power train these preliminary battery configurations could not meet all requirements. For the NCA and the NMC cells the maximum allowed battery current was exceeded while the LFP based battery configuration was not able to provide sufficient energy during the take-off and climb phase. For all battery types the number of cells connected in parallel had to be increased to meet all requirements. This resulted in a second set of final battery configurations for the different cell types that could provide the required power throughout the flight profile without exceeding any limits.

According to literature [9,56], a flight reserve of 19 % battery SOC is required after take-off and climb. The SOC after take-off and climb for the final battery configurations was examined and all systems showed a sufficiently high SOC after take-off and climb. However, when simulating an extended flight profile that included a go around after a failed landing attempt, the allowed fuel cell current in the system containing the battery configuration based on the LFP battery cells was slightly exceeded.

The battery losses for the configuration with the different battery types were also examined. Due to the different current distribution as well as the different current- voltage behaviors of the battery types the losses for the three final configurations differed. The system based on the LFP cells showed the highest losses, followed by the NCA based system, while the NMC had the lowest losses of all.

When comparing the weights of the three final battery systems based on the different cell types, it is found that the NCA base configuration weighted less than the NMC based solution, despite the fact that the NMC single cells had the highest specific energy on cell level. Due to the voltage and current restraints and the specific behavior of the cells more cells of the NMC type were required than for the NCA system resulting in the higher weight on system level. The required battery system based on LFP cells is the heaviest of all three options.

The comparison of the different battery types and the necessary configuration (number of cells in series and in parallel) for each type showed that the lightest cell on cell level is not necessary the lightest on system level. Despite the fact that the NMC cells, which were pouch cells, had the highest specific energy on cell level, they did not proof to be the best suited on system level for this hybrid system. Due to voltage and power constraints, as well as flight reserve requirements, a larger number of cells (in total 720 battery cells) was required for the NMC cells for the examined power train and power demand profile than for the battery system based on NCA cells (in total 400 cells) and the weight for the NMC system was around 9 % higher with 200.2 kg compared to 182.9 kg for the NCA based system. The results also demonstrate that in order to optimize performance as well as weight a detailed analysis of the battery system configuration and behavior is necessary.

In future the influence on the system due to variations in cell performance like deviations in voltage or capacity in one cell type could be examined. The model could then be expanded to implement and test system measures like cell balancing. The model could also be expanded to include a thermal model and a suitable cooling system.

The inclusion of additional cells for example with higher capacity could also be done in the future since a configuration that requires less cells and therefore less connections, busbars and sensors offer the possibility of weight reduction. The inclusion of other cell types like all-solid state, lithium‑sulfur, lithium-air or post lithium batteries should be considered in future work to assess their suitability for aircraft applications.

Pia Hoenicke: Writing – original draft, Visualization, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Caroline Willich: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Supervision, Conceptualization.
The authors thank Ulm University, GE Aerospace as well as the German Research Foundation (DFG) within the project Polis (Project ID 390874152), for funding parts of this work.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors have no conflict of interest to declare.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors thank Roni Kathri for performing parts of the battery measurements mentioned in this work, as well as Christiane Bauer and Mohamed Osama for their advice with regards to the battery model.
